description: Fundamental analysis framework for stock selection. Always use Consolidated figures on Screener.in. All checks cover the last 5 years.

stock_universe.note: Only consider stocks priced above ₹500. Screen from Nifty 50 first, then expand to Nifty 100 and Nifty 200.

criteria.name: ROCE — Return on Capital Employed
criteria.where: Capital Employed = Total Assets − Current Liabilities

threshold.consistency: must exceed 15% in ALL 5 years — one bad year is a warning, two bad years is a reject

criteria.reject_example: ROCE: 22%, 10%, 11%, 21%, 24% → REJECT (two or more years below 15%)
criteria.why_it_matters: ROCE above 15% means the company earns more than its cost of capital. Consistently high ROCE signals a durable competitive advantage.

criteria.name: EPS — Earnings Per Share
criteria.formula: Net Profit After Tax / Total Shares Outstanding

threshold.detail: EPS must be positive in all 5 years OR show a clear upward trend. If EPS is zero or negative in ANY single year out of 5 — REJECT immediately.

criteria.hard_reject_rule: EPS = 0 or EPS < 0 in any year → REJECT, no exceptions
criteria.pass_example: EPS: 12, 14, 13, 17, 20 → PASS (positive trend, slight dip in year 3 is acceptable)
criteria.why_it_matters: Zero or negative EPS means the company is not profitable. A positional investor cannot rely on a business that loses money even for a single year.

criteria.name: FCF — Free Cash Flow
criteria.lookup_on_screener: Cash Flow Statement → Cash from Operations − Capex (or use FCF directly if available)

threshold.detail: FCF must show an increasing trend over 5 years. Minor year-on-year dips are acceptable if the overall slope is upward.

criteria.hard_reject_rule: If FCF is negative in 3 or more years out of 5 — REJECT
criteria.pass_example: FCF (Cr): 200, 250, 230, 310, 400 → PASS (positive slope, one small dip acceptable)
criteria.warn_example: FCF (Cr): 200, 250, -50, 310, 400 → WARN (one negative year — investigate reason, e.g. major capex cycle)
criteria.reject_example: FCF (Cr): 200, -100, -150, 310, -200 → REJECT (3 negative years out of 5)
criteria.why_it_matters: FCF shows actual cash generated after reinvestment. Consistently negative FCF means the company is burning cash and cannot self-fund growth.

criteria.name: Interest Coverage Ratio
criteria.where: PBIT = Profit Before Interest and Tax
criteria.lookup_on_screener: Profit & Loss → calculate as: (Net Profit + Tax + Interest) / Interest

threshold.bad_example: PBIT = 100, Interest = 150 → Coverage = 0.67× (BAD — company cannot service debt from operations)
threshold.danger_zone: Below 1.5× in any year is a serious red flag

criteria.hard_reject_rule: Interest Coverage below 1.0 in any year → REJECT (company cannot cover interest from profits)
criteria.warn_example: Coverage: 8×, 10×, 3×, 12×, 15× → WARN (one weak year — investigate debt spike or one-off)
criteria.reject_example: Coverage: 8×, 10×, 0.8×, 12×, 15× → REJECT (below 1× means interest exceeds PBIT)
criteria.why_it_matters: A low interest coverage ratio means a large portion of operating profit goes to lenders, leaving little for shareholders and growth.

criteria.name: Net Margin
criteria.lookup_on_screener: Key Metrics → Net Profit % or calculate from P&L

threshold.detail: Net Margin must be positive in all 5 years AND the 5-year CAGR of Net Margin must be positive (i.e. margins are expanding, not shrinking)

criteria.warn_example: Net Margin: 12%, 11%, 10%, 9%, 8% → WARN (shrinking margins — negative CAGR)
criteria.reject_example: Net Margin: 12%, 11%, -2%, 9%, 8% → REJECT (negative margin in one year)
criteria.why_it_matters: Expanding net margins signal pricing power and cost discipline. Shrinking margins over 5 years mean competition or inflation is eroding profitability.

criteria.name: Economic Moat
criteria.description: The company must have at least one identifiable and durable competitive advantage (moat) that protects its market position and profitability from competition.

moats.description: Customers repeatedly choose this company's product over cheaper alternatives purely due to brand trust or perception.
Customers face high switching costs (financial, technical, or operational) if they move to a competitor. Also called Switching Cost Moat.
The company produces goods or services at a structurally lower cost than competitors due to scale, process, or location advantages, allowing it to undercut or earn higher margins.
The product or service becomes more valuable as more users join the network, making it very hard for new entrants to displace. Also called Network Effect Moat.

how_to_assess: Read the company's latest annual report — Management Discussion section
Check if pricing power has been maintained over 5 years (prices raised without losing customers)
Check if market share has been stable or growing despite competitors entering
Look at ROCE trend — a high and stable ROCE (>15%) itself is evidence of a moat
criteria.reject_rule: If the company has no identifiable moat and operates in a pure commodity business with no differentiation — REJECT or downweight significantly
criteria.why_it_matters: A moat determines whether today's high ROCE will persist. Without a moat, competition erodes margins and ROCE converges to average over time.

criteria.name: Dividends
criteria.formula: Dividend Per Share (DPS) paid per year
criteria.lookup_on_screener: Key Metrics → Dividend Yield or Profit & Loss → Dividend Payout

threshold.detail: Dividend must be declared and paid in every single year of the last 5 years. Even one year with zero dividend is a reject signal.

criteria.hard_reject_rule: Dividend = 0 in any year out of 5 → REJECT
criteria.fail_example: DPS (Rs): 5, 6, 0, 8, 10 → REJECT (zero dividend in year 3 — company withheld dividend, signals cash stress or management priority change)
criteria.note: The absolute dividend amount does not need to grow every year, but it must never be zero. Growing dividends are a strong positive signal.
criteria.why_it_matters: Consistent dividends signal that the company generates surplus cash reliably and that management respects shareholder returns. A dividend cut or skip is often an early warning of deteriorating business health.

overall_decision.AVOID: Any hard REJECT triggered on E1 (EPS), D (Dividend), or I (Interest Coverage below 1×)
overall_decision.REJECT: Hard reject on FCF (3+ negative years) or ROCE (2+ years below 15%) or any single hard reject rule

checklist_order: Step 1 — Check Stock Universe: Is the stock in Nifty 50 / Nifty 100 / Nifty 200 AND priced above ₹500?
Step 2 — Open Screener.in → Search stock → Switch to Consolidated view
Step 3 — R: Check ROCE for last 5 years. All above 15%?
Step 4 — E1: Check EPS for last 5 years. Any zero or negative? If yes → REJECT immediately
Step 5 — F: Check FCF for last 5 years. Positive slope? 3 or more negative years? If yes → REJECT
Step 6 — I: Calculate Interest Coverage (PBIT / Interest) for last 5 years. Any year below 1×? If yes → REJECT
Step 7 — N: Check Net Margin for last 5 years. Is the 5-year CAGR of Net Margin positive?
Step 8 — E2: Identify the company's economic moat. At least one of the 4 types must be clearly present
Step 9 — D: Check Dividends for last 5 years. Any year with zero dividend? If yes → REJECT
Step 10 — Score: Apply scoring rules and make final decision
common_mistakes_to_avoid: Never use Standalone figures — always use Consolidated on Screener
Do not skip the FCF check just because Net Profit is positive — a company can show profit but burn cash
Do not accept a stock just because it paid a large one-time special dividend — check regular annual dividends
Interest Coverage below 3× in a capital-intensive industry is a warning even if above 1×
Economic Moat is qualitative — do not skip it because all financial metrics look good
A stock priced below ₹500 is outside the universe — do not analyse it regardless of other factors
